does: This paper develops ARSM-Agent, a full-link security enhancement framework for medical decision-making agents that integrates input risk perception, medical evidence constraints, knowledge consistency verification, decision confidence reweighting, security output control, and adversarial feedback mechanisms. The method combines four loss components—decision accuracy, adversarial robustness, safety refusal, and knowledge consistency—with specified weights in a multi-module collaborative architecture. The framework is evaluated against multiple attack types including semantic perturbation, prompt injection, drug-name confusion, and false-evidence attacks, achieving an 8.7% overall attack success rate and 0.91 knowledge consistency score when compared to baseline approaches including standard LLM-Agent, Retrieval-Agent, Filter-Agent, and Adv-Train-Agent variants.
connects: This work extends prior research on adversarial robustness in language models and agent systems by addressing the specific domain of medical decision-making where safety and trustworthiness are critical. It builds on existing adversarial training methodologies and retrieval-augmented generation approaches while incorporating domain-specific constraints through medical evidence grounding. The multi-loss optimization framework combines ideas from robust machine learning with safety-focused objectives similar to those used in reinforcement learning from human feedback. The paper responds to growing concerns about deploying LLM-based agents in high-stakes medical settings where adversarial attacks and hallucinations pose real risks to patient outcomes.
enables: This framework enables development of more trustworthy medical AI agents deployable in clinical settings by establishing systematic approaches to adversarial robustness verification and knowledge grounding. The modular architecture allows future work to extend individual components, such as incorporating additional types of medical attacks or refining evidence retrieval mechanisms. The methodology could inspire similar security enhancement frameworks in other high-stakes domains beyond medicine. Further research could explore dynamic weight adaptation, incorporation of causal reasoning, integration with clinical decision support systems, and evaluation on larger-scale medical datasets or real clinical workflows to establish broader validation of the approach's effectiveness in practice.
